Category: --- PROJECT IDENTITY: project type, sector, work type, and program classification. The attributes that define what a project IS. ---

Description: Primary functional use of the constructed facility — the single most impactful attribute for benchmarking. A $50M hospital and a $50M warehouse have completely different cost profiles, risk patterns, and trade compositions. Custom values are common across companies — normalization to a standard taxonomy is critical for cross-company comparison.
Industry Standard: CSI UniFormat; RS Means Project Type; ENR Classification
Common Values: Commercial Office, Retail, Healthcare (Hospital / Medical Office / Ambulatory), Education (K-12 / Higher Ed), Industrial (Manufacturing / Warehouse / Distribution), Residential (Multi-Family / Single-Family / Senior Living), Hospitality (Hotel / Resort), Government / Civic, Religious, Transportation (Airport / Rail / Transit), Infrastructure (Roads / Bridges / Utilities), Water / Wastewater, Power / Energy, Data Center, Laboratory / Research, Sports / Entertainment, Mixed Use, Renovation / TI
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel; Owner capital planning systems
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field, widely populated (80%+ adoption). Free-text with common patterns, but no enforced picklist — normalization needed for benchmarking
Benchmarking Use: Primary segmentation dimension — most benchmarks start here. Compare any metric by type.
Notes: Most impactful single attribute. Custom values are common — companies use their own naming. Normalization is essential for cross-company analysis.

Description: Further classification within primary project type — customer-defined refinement. Examples: 'Class A Office', 'Acute Care', 'Fulfillment Center', 'Garden-Style Apartments', 'Charter School'. No industry standard taxonomy for subtypes — each company defines their own.
Common Values: Class A Office, Acute Care, Fulfillment Center, Garden-Style Apartments, Charter School, Strip Mall, Ambulatory Surgery Center
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Owner capital planning systems
Procore Coverage: Low — free-text custom field when present; no standardized subtype field in most PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Secondary segmentation — refines type-level benchmarks when enough data exists within a subtype
Notes: No industry standard for subtypes. Only useful when a company has 20+ projects of the same subtype to enable meaningful comparison.

Description: Public vs. private funding and ownership — determines procurement rules, bonding requirements, prevailing wage, and compliance burden. Public projects have transparency requirements and standardized procurement. Private projects move faster with fewer constraints. P3 (Public-Private Partnership) is a growing hybrid model.
Industry Standard: Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR); State procurement codes; Miller Act
Common Values: Private, Public – Federal, Public – State, Public – Local / Municipal, Institutional, Non-Profit, Public-Private Partnership (P3)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; Owner capital planning systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — no dedicated sector field in most PM systems; often inferred from owner type or tracked via custom fields
Benchmarking Use: Segment by procurement rules and compliance burden — public projects have different cost and schedule profiles
Notes: Public projects have prevailing wage, bonding, and procurement requirements that add 10-30% to labor cost. P3 is growing.

Description: New construction vs. renovation vs. addition — fundamentally different risk and cost profiles. Renovation projects have 2-3x the change order rate of new construction due to unknown existing conditions. Mixing work types in benchmarks produces misleading results. Must be filtered before any cost or schedule comparison.
Common Values: New Construction, Renovation / Remodel, Addition, Tenant Improvement / Fit-Out, Demolition, Historic Restoration, Infrastructure Rehabilitation, Adaptive Reuse
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — no dedicated work type field in most PM systems; often embedded in project name or tracked via custom fields
Benchmarking Use: Filters benchmarks — renovation has fundamentally different cost and risk profiles from new construction
Notes: Renovation projects have 2-3x change order rates. Existing conditions discovery is the #1 source of changes. Always filter by work type before benchmarking.

Description: Owner's capital program classification — groups projects by investment purpose across a portfolio. Useful for owners running rollout or prototype programs (retail chains, QSR, healthcare clinics) where standardized scope enables tight benchmarking. Customer-defined with no industry standard.
Common Values: Capital Improvement, Maintenance & Repair, Expansion, Deferred Maintenance, Prototype Rollout, New Market Entry, Sustainability Retrofit
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Owner capital planning systems; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — custom field when present; most companies don't track program classification in PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Portfolio-level analysis — groups projects by investment purpose for program-level benchmarking
Notes: Rollout / prototype programs (retail, QSR, healthcare clinics) have distinct benchmarking needs. Standardized scope enables tighter comparison.

Category: --- FINANCIAL: project value, cost structure, and financial benchmarking attributes. ---

Description: Owner's initial budget or estimated total project cost at authorization — the 'what we thought it would cost' baseline. Set before contracts are awarded. Different from contract value (which is the GC agreement). Used to measure cost growth from initial estimate through final completion.
Common Values: $0 – $10B+ (wide range by type: $500K TI to $5B+ mega-project)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera; MS Excel; Owner budgeting systems
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field (total_value or estimated_value), widely populated
Benchmarking Use: Baseline for cost growth analysis — compare initial estimate to contract value to final cost
Notes: Often the number used when the project is first created. May be a rough estimate (Class 5) or detailed estimate (Class 1). Precision matters for benchmarking.

Description: Contracted amount between owner and GC/CM — the GMP, lump sum, or negotiated target. This is the number subcontractors and the market see. Updated via change orders over the project lifecycle. Represents the GC's contractual obligation.
Common Values: $0 – $10B+ (GMP, lump sum, or negotiated)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field, widely populated. Change order tracking captures modifications to contract value
Benchmarking Use: Primary cost benchmark dimension — defines project scale band
Notes: GMP, lump sum, or negotiated. May differ significantly from estimated value. Change orders modify this over time.

Description: Bucketed range for benchmarking by project scale — log-scale bands work better than linear because construction costs, risks, and outcomes don't scale linearly. A $100M project is not simply 10x a $10M project — it's a fundamentally different kind of project.
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — derived from total value or contract value, which are widely populated
Benchmarking Use: Core benchmarking dimension — project scale bands normalize comparison across project sizes
Notes: Band thresholds should align with industry norms and data distribution. Log-scale bands work better than linear.

Description: Total project cost divided by gross building area — the most comparable unit cost metric across building projects. Only meaningful for building projects (not infrastructure). Varies enormously by type: warehouse $100-200/SF vs. hospital $600-1500/SF. Must normalize by type and region.
Industry Standard: RS Means Square Foot Cost Data; ENR Cost Data
Common Values: $100–$1,500+/SF depending on type, region, and complexity
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires both cost and area fields populated; area field is often empty or inconsistent (gross vs. net)
Benchmarking Use: Key unit cost benchmark — most comparable metric across building projects
Notes: Only meaningful for building projects. Varies enormously by type and region. Must normalize by type, region, and year.

Description: Direct construction cost excluding soft costs (design fees, permits, FF&E, land, financing). Typically 60-80% of total project cost. Isolates construction cost from owner's total investment. Essential for apples-to-apples cost benchmarking.
Industry Standard: AACE Recommended Practice 10S-90; CSI Uniformat Level 1
Common Values: 60-80% of total project cost (varies by delivery method and owner cost structure)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Owner accounting systems; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — most PM systems track total contract value, not hard cost vs. soft cost breakdown. Requires owner-side data or custom fields
Benchmarking Use: Isolates construction cost from owner's total project cost for cleaner benchmarking
Notes: Hard cost vs. total cost distinction matters. Some customers track only hard cost; others include soft costs. Always clarify definition.

Description: Owner and/or GC contingency allowance at project start — the risk reserve. Owner contingency covers scope changes; GC contingency covers construction risks. How fast contingency burns is a leading indicator of project risk. Separate tracking of owner vs. GC contingency is best practice.
Common Values: 3-10% of contract value (varies by project risk profile: 3% for simple, 10% for complex renovation)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Owner accounting systems
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated contingency field in most PM systems; tracked in budget line items or custom fields
Benchmarking Use: Contingency utilization rate is a key risk metric — fast burn = project in trouble
Notes: Separate owner contingency from GC contingency. Owner contingency covers scope; GC covers construction risk.

Description: Actual total cost when project is complete — includes all change orders, allowance adjustments, and final settlements. The gap between contract value and final cost is the most important financial performance indicator. Only available after project completion.
Common Values: $0 – $10B+ (final cost = contract value + all approved changes)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Owner accounting systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires project to be complete and all financial data closed out. Financial module captures final budget vs. committed
Benchmarking Use: Cost growth = (Final - Contract) / Contract — the core financial performance metric
Notes: Only available after project completion. Average cost growth is 4-8% for well-managed projects, 15-25% for poorly managed or complex renovations.

Category: --- LOCATION: geographic location, climate zone, and seismic characteristics. ---

Description: Street address of the project site — source for all derived geographic attributes. Geocoding converts address to latitude/longitude for distance, climate zone, and cost index calculations. Not directly used in benchmarks but foundational for derived attributes.
Common Values: Street address, city, state, ZIP
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project address fields, widely populated
Benchmarking Use: Not directly benchmarked — enables derived geographic attributes via geocoding
Notes: Source for all derived geographic attributes. Quality of address data varies — some projects use office address instead of site address.

Description: City or municipality where the project is located — enables local market analysis. Labor rates, material costs, and subcontractor availability vary significantly by metro area. City-level benchmarks useful when enough project volume exists within a metro.
Common Values: Any city name (top metros: New York, Los Angeles, Chicago, Houston, Phoenix, Dallas, etc.)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field, widely populated
Benchmarking Use: Local market analysis — labor rates and material costs vary by metro area
Notes: City-level benchmarks useful for large metros with enough project volume. Suburban vs. urban within a metro also matters.

Description: State or province jurisdiction — major benchmarking dimension. State drives labor law (prevailing wage, right-to-work), building code edition, licensing requirements, and tax structure. Top 20 US states cover 80%+ of commercial construction volume.
Common Values: US states, Canadian provinces, etc.
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field, widely populated
Benchmarking Use: Regional benchmarking dimension — top 20 states cover 80%+ of US commercial construction
Notes: State drives prevailing wage, right-to-work, building code edition, and licensing. Major benchmarking dimension.

Description: Country where the project is located — relevant for international benchmarking. Most construction data models are US-centric. International projects require currency normalization and different code/standard references.
Common Values: US, Canada, UK, Australia, etc.
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field, widely populated
Benchmarking Use: International benchmarking requires currency and standard normalization
Notes: Most construction analytics are US-centric. International expansion requires localized cost indices and code frameworks.

Description: Postal code of the project site — enables micro-market analysis and cost index application. RS Means and other cost databases use ZIP code for location cost factors. ZIP-level granularity useful for labor market and subcontractor availability analysis.
Industry Standard: USPS ZIP Code system; RS Means City Cost Index
Common Values: Standard postal format (US: 5-digit or ZIP+4)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field, widely populated. ZIP code enables automated cost index lookup
Benchmarking Use: Enables micro-market analysis and location cost factor application
Notes: RS Means and other cost databases index by ZIP. Location cost factors range from 0.7 (rural South) to 1.4 (Manhattan).

Description: ASHRAE or IECC climate zone based on project location — drives envelope design, energy code compliance, and expected weather delay days. Zone 1 (hot-humid) to Zone 8 (subarctic) have fundamentally different design and construction requirements. Critical for HVAC and enclosure benchmarking.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Standard 169; IECC Climate Zone Map
Common Values: Zone 1A through Zone 8 (ASHRAE 169); Zone 1–8 (IECC). Examples: Miami = 1A, Houston = 2A, Atlanta = 3A, New York = 4A, Chicago = 5A, Minneapolis = 6A
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; ASHRAE; DOE Climate Zone tools
Procore Coverage: None — not a standard field in PM systems. Derived from project location (ZIP code or city). Can be calculated automatically from address
Benchmarking Use: Compare energy cost, enclosure design, and weather delay risk by climate zone
Notes: Climate zone drives envelope R-value, HVAC sizing, and expected weather delay days. Can be auto-derived from location.

Description: ASCE 7 seismic classification based on site soil conditions and proximity to faults. Higher SDC = more expensive structure and connections. Significant cost impact in SDC D and above — affects structural design, fire protection (seismic bracing), and MEP (flexible connections).
Common Values: A (lowest), B, C, D0, D1, D2, E, F (highest). Most of California and Pacific NW = D or higher
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; USGS Seismic Hazard Maps; Structural engineering tools
Procore Coverage: None — not a standard field in PM systems. Derived from site location and soil conditions. Requires geotechnical data or USGS lookup
Benchmarking Use: Compare structural cost premium by seismic design category
Notes: SDC D+ adds 5-15% to structural cost. Seismic bracing for MEP is an additional cost often missed in early estimates.

Description: Geographic region for market-level analysis — aggregates state-level data for regional trends. Region definitions vary by organization but should align with labor market and cost index boundaries. Typically 8-10 regions sufficient for US benchmarking.
Industry Standard: ENR Regional Cost Data; BLS Regions
Common Values: Northeast, Southeast, Midwest, Southwest, Mountain West, Pacific Northwest, Pacific Southwest, Gulf Coast, Mid-Atlantic, Great Plains
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; ENR; RS Means; BLS
Procore Coverage: Low — not typically a standard field; derived from state or ZIP. Custom field when tracked
Benchmarking Use: Aggregates state-level data into regional market trends
Notes: Region definitions vary by organization. Should align with labor market boundaries and cost index regions.

Category: --- SCHEDULE: timeline, duration, and schedule performance attributes. ---

Description: Scheduled construction start date — typically Notice to Proceed (NTP) or mobilization date. The contractual baseline against which all schedule performance is measured. Some owners define this as first day of site work; others use NTP date.
Common Values: ISO 8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Project; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field (planned start date or target start date), widely populated
Benchmarking Use: Baseline for schedule performance measurement
Notes: NTP is most common definition. Variance between planned and actual start captures pre-construction delays.

Description: Actual date construction work began on site — may differ from planned start due to permitting, financing, or design delays. The gap between planned and actual start captures pre-construction schedule risk.
Common Values: ISO 8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Project; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field (actual_start_date), populated when work begins
Benchmarking Use: Schedule performance = Actual Start vs. Planned Start — captures pre-construction delays
Notes: Variance between planned and actual start captures permitting, financing, and design delays.

Description: Contractual substantial completion date — when the owner can occupy the facility. Different from final completion (all punch items done, all contracts closed). Most contracts use substantial completion as the contractual milestone. Liquidated damages typically attach to this date.
Common Values: ISO 8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Project; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project field (projected_finish_date or target completion), widely populated
Benchmarking Use: Baseline for schedule growth analysis
Notes: Substantial completion (owner can occupy) vs. final completion (all punch done, all contracts closed). Most contracts use substantial.

Description: Actual date of substantial completion — only available when the project reaches that milestone. The gap between contractual and actual completion is the core schedule performance metric. Average schedule growth is 10-20% for well-managed projects.
Common Values: ISO 8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Project; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — dedicated project field (actual_completion_date) exists but only populated on completed projects
Benchmarking Use: Schedule growth = (Actual Duration - Planned Duration) / Planned Duration — core schedule metric
Notes: Only available when project completes. Average schedule growth is 10-20%. Renovation projects have higher growth.

Description: Contractual construction duration in calendar days from NTP to substantial completion. Industry typically uses calendar days for overall duration (not working days). Excludes pre-construction. Duration is strongly correlated with project type and value band.
Common Values: 2–60+ months (typical: 6-24 months for commercial, 24-48 for healthcare, 36-60 for infrastructure)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Project; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — derived from planned start and completion dates. Calendar days vs. working days not always specified
Benchmarking Use: Duration benchmark by project type and value band
Notes: Calendar days vs. working days must be specified. Industry uses calendar days for overall duration.

Description: Actual construction duration from start to substantial completion — compare to planned duration for schedule performance, compare to benchmarks by type/size for relative performance.
Common Values: 2–60+ months
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Project; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — derived from actual start and completion dates. Only available on completed projects
Benchmarking Use: Duration growth is a key schedule performance metric
Notes: Compare to planned for growth. Compare to type/size benchmarks for relative performance.

Description: Time from design authorization to NTP — often ignored but significantly affects construction outcomes. Design-build and CMAR compress this phase. DBB has the longest preconstruction. Longer preconstruction correlates with more complete documents and fewer field changes (to a point).
Industry Standard: AIA B101 (Design Services); AGC CMAR Guidelines
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; Owner systems; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — not a standard field in PM systems. Requires separate tracking of design phase milestones
Benchmarking Use: Preconstruction efficiency metric — correlates with document quality and field change rate
Notes: Design-build compresses preconstruction but shifts risk. Longer preconstruction often means fewer surprises in the field.

Description: Season when construction begins — affects early work scope and weather delay risk. Winter starts in cold climates (ASHRAE zones 5-8) face frozen ground, concrete cold weather protection, and shorter daylight hours. Correlates with higher sitework cost.
Common Values: Spring (Mar-May), Summer (Jun-Aug), Fall (Sep-Nov), Winter (Dec-Feb)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — derived from actual or planned start date. Not a stored field but easily calculated
Benchmarking Use: Weather risk proxy — winter starts in cold climates have higher delay risk and sitework cost
Notes: Winter starts in climate zones 5-8 face frozen ground, concrete protection, and shorter days. Can be auto-derived from start date.

Category: --- PROCUREMENT: delivery method, contract type, and procurement approach. How the project is organized, contracted, and bought out. ---

Description: Project delivery system defining the design-construction relationship — one of the strongest predictors of project outcomes. DBB separates design and construction (owner bears coordination risk). CMAR/GMP brings the contractor in during design (shared risk). DB puts design and construction under one contract (contractor bears risk). IPD shares risk and reward among all parties.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 / A133 / A141; ConsensusDocs; DBIA; AGC Delivery Methods
Common Values: Design-Bid-Build (DBB), Construction Manager at Risk (CMAR / GMP), Design-Build (DB), CM as Agent (CMa), Integrated Project Delivery (IPD), Multi-Prime, Progressive Design-Build, Bridging
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel; Owner capital planning systems
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated delivery method field in most PM systems. Custom field when tracked. Often inferred from contract type
Benchmarking Use: Major benchmarking dimension — delivery method affects cost growth, schedule, and change order rate
Notes: CMAR/GMP most common for large commercial. DBB dominant in public. DB growing in infrastructure. IPD rare but lowest change rate.

Description: Compensation structure between owner and GC/CM — determines who bears cost risk. GMP: GC bears overrun risk above the guaranteed maximum. Lump Sum: GC bears all cost risk. Cost Plus: owner bears cost risk. Contract type determines who benefits from efficiency and who absorbs overruns.
Industry Standard: AIA A101 (Lump Sum); AIA A133 (GMP); ConsensusDocs
Common Values: Lump Sum / Stipulated Sum, Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP), Cost Plus Fixed Fee, Cost Plus Percentage, Time & Materials (T&M), Unit Price, Target Price with Shared Savings
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated contract type field in most PM systems. Custom field when tracked
Benchmarking Use: Affects risk allocation and financial performance patterns — GMP vs. Lump Sum vs. Cost Plus have different change dynamics
Notes: GMP: GC bears overrun risk. Lump Sum: GC bears all risk. Cost Plus: owner bears risk. Dramatically affects financial benchmarks.

Description: How the contractor was selected — affects pricing competitiveness and project relationship dynamics. Open competitive typically yields lowest initial price but highest change order rate. Negotiated/QBS (Qualifications-Based Selection) correlates with lower total cost at completion.
Industry Standard: Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR); Brooks Act (QBS for A/E); State procurement codes
Common Values: Competitive Bid (Open), Competitive Bid (Invited / Select), Negotiated, Sole Source / Direct Award, Best Value (Price + Qualifications), Qualifications-Based Selection (QBS)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; Owner procurement systems; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Procurement data lives in owner or pre-construction systems
Benchmarking Use: Affects pricing and total cost — open competitive has lower initial price but higher change rate
Notes: Open competitive yields lowest bid but often highest final cost. Negotiated/QBS yields higher initial price but lower total cost.

Description: Whether performance and payment bonds are required — adds 1-3% to cost and limits the contractor pool to firms with adequate bonding capacity. Federal projects over $250K require bonds (Miller Act). Many state and local public projects have similar requirements.
Industry Standard: Miller Act (Federal); State Little Miller Acts; AIA A312 (Bond Forms)
Common Values: Fully Bonded (Performance + Payment), Payment Bond Only, No Bond Required, Subcontractor Bonds Required
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; Surety companies; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Bond information lives in contract administration or surety systems
Benchmarking Use: Bonding adds 1-3% to cost — affects pricing and limits which contractors can bid
Notes: Public projects over $250K require bonds (Miller Act). Bonding capacity limits contractor pool. Bond cost passed to owner.

Description: Whether prevailing wage requirements apply — adds 10-30% to labor cost depending on market differential between prevailing and market rates. Federal (Davis-Bacon) and state prevailing wage laws apply to public projects. Some private projects trigger prevailing wage through public incentives or tax credits.
Industry Standard: Davis-Bacon Act (Federal); State prevailing wage laws
Common Values: Yes, No
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; DOL Wage Determinations
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Wage determination data lives in payroll/compliance systems
Benchmarking Use: Prevailing wage adds 10-30% to labor cost — must be filtered for cost benchmarking
Notes: Federal and state prevailing wage laws apply to public projects. Some private projects trigger it through public incentives.

Description: Labor agreement type on the project — affects labor rates, productivity assumptions, and trade jurisdiction rules. Union projects have higher hourly rates but potentially different productivity. Project Labor Agreements (PLAs) common on large public projects. Trade jurisdiction rules affect crew composition and flexibility.
Common Values: Union (Closed Shop), Open Shop, Project Labor Agreement (PLA), Mixed
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Payroll systems
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Labor agreement information lives in contract or payroll systems
Benchmarking Use: Affects labor rates and productivity — must normalize for union vs. open shop in cost benchmarks
Notes: Union projects have higher hourly rates. PLAs common on large public projects. Trade jurisdiction rules affect crew composition.

Category: --- BUILDING: physical characteristics, structural system, code classification, and occupancy. ---

Description: Total enclosed floor area including all floors — the primary size dimension for cost benchmarking. Gross area includes occupied, mechanical, circulation, and wall space. Different from rentable or usable area. Most important physical attribute for cost-per-SF calculations.
Industry Standard: ASTM E1836 (Standard Classification for Building Floor Area); BOMA
Common Values: 500 – 10M+ SF (typical commercial: 10K-500K SF)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; BIM (Revit); MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — project area field exists but inconsistently populated. No enforcement of gross vs. net vs. rentable definition
Benchmarking Use: Primary size dimension — most cost-per-SF calculations use gross building area
Notes: Definition matters: gross, net, rentable, usable are different numbers. Ensure apples-to-apples when benchmarking cost/SF.

Description: Count of floors above ground level — affects structural system, vertical transportation requirements, fire protection, and egress. High-rise (typically 7+ stories or 75+ ft) triggers different code requirements for structure, egress, and fire protection. Each additional floor adds elevator, stair, and structural cost premium.
Common Values: 1–100+ (low-rise: 1-3, mid-rise: 4-7, high-rise: 7+)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; BIM (Revit); MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — project field exists but not always populated. High-rise designation derived from height and stories
Benchmarking Use: Affects structural system, vertical transportation, and code requirements — key cost driver
Notes: High-rise (7+ stories / 75+ ft) requires different structural systems, fire protection, and egress. Each floor adds cost premium.

Description: Count of floors below ground level (basement levels) — below-grade construction costs 1.5-3x above-grade per SF due to excavation, shoring, waterproofing, and dewatering. Parking structures below grade are common in urban settings.
Common Values: 0–5+ (most common: 0-2)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; BIM (Revit); MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — not a standard field in most PM systems. Sometimes captured in project description or custom fields
Benchmarking Use: Below-grade construction adds significant cost premium — 1.5-3x above-grade per SF
Notes: Below-grade space: excavation, shoring, waterproofing, dewatering. Underground parking is $50-80K/space.

Description: Height from grade to highest occupied floor or roof peak — triggers high-rise code requirements (typically 75+ ft). Determines crane type (tower vs. mobile), wind load design, and elevator requirements.
Common Values: 10–2000+ ft (low-rise: <75 ft, high-rise: 75+)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; BIM (Revit); MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — not a standard field in most PM systems. Sometimes in project description
Benchmarking Use: Affects structural system, crane requirements, and code classification
Notes: Height triggers high-rise code requirements at 75+ ft. Determines crane type. Affects wind load design.

Description: Total area of the project site — affects logistics, staging, and site improvement cost. Tight urban sites increase logistics cost due to limited staging, laydown, and equipment access. Large sites increase paving, utilities, and landscaping scope.
Common Values: 0.1–1000+ acres (urban infill: 0.1-1, suburban: 1-20, campus: 20-100)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; GIS; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — not a standard field in most PM systems. Sometimes captured in project description or derived from site plan
Benchmarking Use: Site size affects logistics cost, staging constraints, and site improvement scope
Notes: Tight urban sites increase logistics cost. Large sites increase scope. Footprint-to-site ratio indicates density.

Description: Primary structural framing system — the single biggest cost and schedule driver for any building. Steel frame: faster erection, lighter foundation. Concrete frame: potentially cheaper for regular grids, longer cycle time. Mass timber: emerging for mid-rise, different fire protection. Choice affects cost, schedule, trade composition, and crane requirements.
Industry Standard: AISC; ACI; AWC NDS; IBC Chapter 19-23
Common Values: Steel Frame, Concrete Frame (CIP), Precast Concrete, Wood Frame (Light), Mass Timber, Masonry Bearing Wall, Tilt-Up Concrete, Hybrid (Steel + Concrete), Post-Tensioned Concrete
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; BIM (Revit/Tekla); MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated structural system field in most PM systems. Custom field or inferred from cost code distribution
Benchmarking Use: Compare structural cost per SF and erection rate by system type — largest single cost driver
Notes: Structural system is the single biggest cost driver. Steel vs. concrete vs. mass timber have fundamentally different cost and schedule profiles.

Description: Primary foundation system — driven by geotechnical conditions, not project type. Deep foundations (driven piles, drilled shafts) add $20-100/SF to substructure cost vs. shallow foundations (spread footings). Foundation type has significant schedule and cost impact in early phases.
Common Values: Spread Footings, Mat / Raft Foundation, Driven Piles, Drilled Shafts (Caissons), Micropiles / Helical Piers, Combined (Shallow + Deep), Slab on Grade Only
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; Geotechnical consultants; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Foundation type lives in geotechnical reports and structural drawings
Benchmarking Use: Compare substructure cost by foundation type and soil conditions
Notes: Foundation type driven by geotechnical conditions. Deep foundations add $20-100/SF. Pile driving has noise/vibration impacts.

Description: Count of individual units — apartments, hotel rooms, patient rooms, beds, or seats. Per-unit cost is the primary benchmark for residential, hospitality, and healthcare. Only applicable for certain building types. Normalizes cost comparison across different building sizes within the same type.
Common Values: 1–5000+ (apartments: 50-500, hotel rooms: 100-1000, hospital beds: 50-500)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; Owner program documents; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — not a standard field in most PM systems. Custom field when tracked
Benchmarking Use: Per-unit cost is the primary benchmark for residential ($/unit) and hospitality ($/key)
Notes: Only applicable for certain types: multi-family, hospitality, healthcare, education. Unit definition varies by type.

Description: Type of parking provided — parking structure can be 15-25% of total project cost. Underground parking is most expensive ($50-80K/space). Structured above-grade: $20-40K/space. Surface: $2-5K/space. Parking type significantly affects total project cost and should be tracked for cost benchmarking.
Common Values: Surface Lot, Structured Garage (Above Grade), Underground Garage, Podium / Pedestal, No Parking, Mechanical / Automated
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Parking type lives in design documents or custom fields
Benchmarking Use: Parking type dramatically affects total cost — underground is 10-20x more expensive per space than surface
Notes: Underground parking is the most expensive ($50-80K/space). Often 15-25% of total project cost.

Description: IBC construction type classification — determines allowable building height and area, required fire-resistance ratings for structural elements, and material restrictions. Type I-A (most fire resistant, most expensive) to Type V-B (least fire resistant, least expensive). Directly drives structural material and fireproofing cost.
Common Values: Type I-A (Fire Resistive / Protected), Type I-B, Type II-A, Type II-B, Type III-A, Type III-B, Type IV-A/B/C (Mass Timber), Type IV-HT (Heavy Timber), Type V-A, Type V-B
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; RS Means; BIM (Revit); Code analysis tools
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Construction type lives in code analysis documents and permit applications
Benchmarking Use: Compare material cost and fire protection requirements by construction type
Notes: Type I-A is most expensive (full fire resistance). Type V-B is cheapest (no fire resistance). Type IV (mass timber) is emerging.

Description: IBC occupancy classification — drives code requirements for occupant load, egress, fire protection, accessibility, and special inspections. I-2 (hospitals) is the most demanding occupancy. Occupancy group determines fire sprinkler requirements, exit width, and structural importance factor.
Common Values: A (Assembly), B (Business), E (Educational), F (Factory), H (High Hazard), I (Institutional), M (Mercantile), R (Residential), S (Storage), U (Utility)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Code analysis tools; RS Means
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Occupancy group lives in code analysis and permit documents
Benchmarking Use: Drives egress, fire protection, accessibility, and special inspection requirements
Notes: I-2 (hospitals) is most demanding. Occupancy group determines sprinkler requirements, exit width, accessibility.

Description: Edition of the building code adopted by the jurisdiction — affects energy, structural, and accessibility requirements. Jurisdictions adopt different editions on different timelines. Newer codes have stricter energy requirements. Some jurisdictions have significant local amendments.
Common Values: IBC 2015, IBC 2018, IBC 2021, IBC 2024, California Building Code, NYC Building Code, Local amendment
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; ICC; Jurisdiction code databases; Code analysis tools
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Building code edition lives in permit application and code analysis documents
Benchmarking Use: Newer code editions have stricter energy requirements — affects MEP and enclosure cost
Notes: Jurisdictions adopt different editions on different timelines. Local amendments can be significant (NYC, CA).

Description: Whether automatic fire sprinklers are required or provided — sprinkler cost is $3-8/SF but allows construction type trade-offs (increased height and area under IBC). Almost all new commercial and multi-family construction is now fully sprinklered. NFPA 13 for commercial, NFPA 13R for residential.
Common Values: Fully Sprinklered (NFPA 13), Partially Sprinklered, No Sprinkler Required, NFPA 13R (Residential), NFPA 13D (One/Two Family)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Code analysis tools; RS Means
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Sprinkler requirement lives in code analysis and fire protection design
Benchmarking Use: Sprinkler cost ($3-8/SF) vs. construction type savings — trade-off analysis
Notes: Sprinklers allow increased height and area under IBC. Trade-off: sprinkler cost vs. structural savings.

Description: Applicable accessibility standard — affects interior layout, restroom count, fixture selection, and door hardware. ADA applies to all commercial and public buildings. Fair Housing Act applies to multi-family (4+ units). State codes often exceed federal ADA requirements.
Industry Standard: ADA; Fair Housing Act (FHA); Section 504; State accessibility codes
Common Values: ADA (Federal), State Accessibility Code, Fair Housing Act (FHA), ADA + State (most common), Section 504 (Federal funding)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Code analysis tools
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Accessibility requirements live in design documents and code analysis
Benchmarking Use: Affects interior layout efficiency, restroom count, and fixture/hardware selection
Notes: ADA applies to all commercial/public. FHA applies to multi-family (4+ units). State codes often exceed ADA.

Category: --- STANDARDS: green building certification and sustainability targets. ---

Description: LEED certification level targeted or achieved — the most widely recognized green building standard. LEED adds 2-8% to construction cost depending on level. Gold is the most common target for institutional and corporate projects. Platinum adds the most cost through high-performance envelope, renewable energy, and water reuse systems.
Common Values: None, LEED Certified, LEED Silver, LEED Gold, LEED Platinum, Pursuing (level TBD)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; USGBC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — custom field when tracked. Not a standard field in most PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Sustainability benchmark — LEED level affects material and system selection cost
Notes: LEED Gold is the most common target for institutional/corporate. Platinum adds most cost. Adds commissioning and documentation requirements.

Description: WELL Building Standard certification level — focuses on occupant health and wellness (air quality, water quality, light, fitness, comfort). Growing adoption in corporate office and multi-family. Adds operational requirements beyond construction. Newer standard than LEED with different focus areas.
Common Values: None, WELL Silver, WELL Gold, WELL Platinum, Pursuing
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; IWBI; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. WELL certification lives in sustainability consultant records
Benchmarking Use: Emerging occupant health standard — growing adoption in corporate office and multi-family
Notes: WELL focuses on air, water, light, fitness, comfort. Adds operational monitoring beyond construction.

Description: Energy code or standard the project must meet — becoming a bigger cost driver as standards tighten. Energy code determines insulation R-values, glazing performance, HVAC efficiency, and lighting power density. Net Zero and Passive House add significant premium (10-25%+).
Common Values: ASHRAE 90.1-2019, ASHRAE 90.1-2022, IECC 2021, IECC 2024, Net Zero, Passive House, Local energy code
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Energy modeling tools (eQUEST, EnergyPlus); RS Means
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Energy code compliance lives in energy models and design documents
Benchmarking Use: Drives MEP system design and envelope performance requirements — increasingly impacts cost
Notes: Energy code is becoming a bigger cost driver as standards tighten. Net Zero adds 10-25% premium. Envelope and HVAC most affected.

Description: Whether the project targets net-zero energy consumption — requires on-site renewable energy (typically solar), high-performance envelope, and ultra-efficient MEP systems. Adds 10-25% to construction cost. Growing requirement from institutional owners and ESG-driven developers.
Industry Standard: DOE Zero Energy Ready Home; ASHRAE 90.1 Appendix G; Architecture 2030
Common Values: Yes, No
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Energy modeling tools; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Net zero target lives in project design criteria
Benchmarking Use: Emerging benchmark for high-performance buildings — adds 10-25% to construction cost
Notes: Net zero requires PV, high-performance envelope, and efficient MEP. Growing requirement from institutional owners.

Description: Special requirements for disaster resilience — essential facilities (hospitals, fire stations, EOCs) must remain operational post-disaster. Requires enhanced structural, MEP, and envelope systems beyond standard code. Flood, hurricane, seismic, blast, and storm shelter requirements each add distinct cost and design requirements.
Industry Standard: ASCE 7 (Risk Categories); FEMA P-361 (Safe Rooms); ICC 500 (Storm Shelters)
Common Values: None, Flood Zone (FEMA), Hurricane / Wind Zone, Seismic (Above Code), Essential Facility (Post-Disaster), Storm Shelter, Blast Resistance
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; FEMA; USGS; RS Means; Structural engineering tools
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Resilience requirements live in design criteria and code analysis
Benchmarking Use: Resilience adds cost but reduces long-term risk — essential facilities have the highest requirements
Notes: Essential facilities (hospitals, fire stations, EOCs) must remain operational post-disaster. Flood zone adds elevation and waterproofing cost.

Category: --- ORGANIZATION: owner, contractors, design team, and workforce characteristics. ---

Description: Type of entity that owns the project — drives procurement method, bonding requirements, and decision-making speed. Private developers prioritize speed and cost. Public owners have procurement rules and transparency requirements. Institutional owners (universities, hospitals) have long approval chains but take a long-term perspective.
Common Values: Private Developer, Private Corporate (Owner-Occupied), Public – Federal, Public – State, Public – Local / Municipal, Institutional (University / Hospital System), Non-Profit, Religious
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; Owner systems; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — can be inferred from company type in vendor/company directory. No dedicated owner type field
Benchmarking Use: Owner type drives procurement method, decision speed, and compliance requirements
Notes: Private developers move fast. Public owners have procurement rules. Institutional owners have long approval chains.

Description: General contractor or construction manager firm name — enables contractor-level performance benchmarking across projects. With enough data: compare change order rates, schedule performance, safety records, and cost outcomes by contractor.
Common Values: Company name (ENR Top 400 contractors, regional firms, local builders)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; ENR Top Lists; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — vendor/company directory tracks GC/CM firm. Project role or contract information identifies the GC
Benchmarking Use: Enables contractor-level performance benchmarking across multiple projects
Notes: Contractor performance benchmarking requires 10+ projects per firm for statistical validity. ENR Top 400 provides industry context.

Description: Architect responsible for design and construction administration — A/E firm performance significantly affects construction outcomes. Firms with higher document quality produce fewer RFIs and change orders. Architect responsiveness during CA affects submittal turnaround.
Common Values: Company name (AIA member firms, regional and national)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — can be identified from project directory or vendor list. No dedicated architect field in most PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Design firm performance — compare RFI volume, change rate, and document quality by A/E firm
Notes: A/E firm performance significantly affects construction outcomes. Document quality correlates inversely with RFI and change order rates.

Description: Total count of subcontractor firms on the project — a proxy for project complexity. More subs = more coordination overhead, more meetings, more potential conflicts. A 100-sub project is fundamentally different from a 20-sub project.
Common Values: 10–200+ (simple: 10-20, typical commercial: 30-60, complex healthcare: 80-200)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — derived from vendor directory or commitment count. Vendor/subcontractor list is well-populated
Benchmarking Use: Proxy for project complexity — more subs = more coordination overhead
Notes: Affected by self-perform strategy. GCs that self-perform more have fewer subs but more direct labor.

Description: Maximum number of workers on site during peak construction — a labor intensity metric. Peak workforce / SF indicates labor density. High peak on a constrained site creates safety and logistics challenges. Peak timing typically aligns with MEP rough-in or interior finishes.
Common Values: 10–5000+ (typical commercial: 50-300, mega-project: 1000-5000)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Daily log manpower records
Procore Coverage: Medium — can be derived from daily log manpower peak headcount. Requires manpower tracking to be active
Benchmarking Use: Labor intensity metric — peak workforce / SF indicates labor density and site congestion
Notes: High peak on constrained site creates safety challenges. Peak timing typically aligns with MEP rough-in or finishes.

Description: Percentage of work self-performed by the GC (not subcontracted) — affects risk profile and cost structure. Most GCs self-perform 5-15% (general conditions, concrete, carpentry). Trade-specific GCs may self-perform 40-60%. Higher self-perform = more direct labor risk but potentially higher margin.
Common Values: 0%–60%+ (most GCs: 5-15%, specialty GCs: 40-60%)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; CFMA benchmarking data
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Self-perform percentage lives in contract or accounting systems
Benchmarking Use: Affects risk profile and margin structure — higher self-perform = more direct labor risk
Notes: Most GCs self-perform 5-15% (GC, concrete, carpentry). Trade-specific GCs self-perform 40-60%.

Category: --- SCOPE & COMPLEXITY: complexity indicators, constraints, and special conditions that affect cost and risk. ---

Description: Whether the project involves existing structures or is entirely new — renovation content fundamentally changes cost and risk. Existing conditions discovery is the #1 source of changes on renovation projects. Asbestos, lead paint, structural deficiency, and hidden utilities are common unknowns. Must be filtered before benchmarking.
Common Values: 100% New Construction, Primarily New with Minor Existing, Mixed New + Renovation, Primarily Renovation with Minor New, 100% Renovation / Remodel
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — not a standard field in most PM systems. Often embedded in project name or work type custom field
Benchmarking Use: Renovation adds complexity and change risk — 2-3x change order rate vs. new construction
Notes: Renovation projects have 2-3x change order rates. Existing conditions discovery is the #1 source of changes.

Description: Whether the building or adjacent spaces are occupied during construction work — adds logistics constraints, safety requirements, work hour restrictions, noise/vibration limits, and security requirements. Adds 10-25% to schedule and significant cost for temporary partitions, phased shutdowns, and after-hours work.
Industry Standard: OSHA Multi-Employer Worksite; ICRA (Infection Control for healthcare)
Common Values: Yes, No
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Occupancy status lives in project description or scope documents
Benchmarking Use: Occupied construction adds logistics constraints, safety requirements, and schedule restrictions
Notes: Adds 10-25% to schedule. Requires temporary partitions, phased shutdowns, and after-hours work. Healthcare ICRA requirements are especially demanding.

Description: Whether the project is built in distinct phases with partial occupancy or turnover between phases. Each phase requires its own punch list, inspections, and turnover. Temporary barriers, life safety systems, and egress must be maintained between phases. Adds remobilization cost and schedule complexity.
Common Values: Yes, No
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Phasing strategy lives in project scope documents
Benchmarking Use: Phased delivery adds remobilization cost and schedule complexity
Notes: Each phase needs its own punch list and inspections. Temporary barriers and life safety between phases add cost.

Description: Whether design and construction overlap — construction starts on early packages (sitework, foundations) while later packages (MEP, interiors) are still being designed. Compresses overall schedule by 15-30% but increases RFI volume and change order rate due to incomplete design information.
Common Values: Yes, No
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Fast-track decision lives in project delivery strategy
Benchmarking Use: Fast-track compresses schedule 15-30% but increases RFI and change rate
Notes: Fast-track means construction starts before design is complete. Higher RFI and change rate. Common with CMAR and DB delivery.

Description: Notable constraints on the construction site — multiple constraints compound and fundamentally change project complexity. An urban constrained site with adjacent occupied buildings and environmental contamination is a different project than a greenfield suburban site. Site constraints drive logistics cost and methodology decisions.
Common Values: Urban / Constrained Site, Adjacent Occupied Buildings, Environmental Contamination, High Water Table, Rock, Historic District, Limited Access / Staging, Noise / Vibration Restrictions, Active Operations, FAA Height Restrictions
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel; Environmental consultants
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Site constraints identified in geotechnical reports and environmental assessments
Benchmarking Use: Site constraints drive logistics cost and methodology decisions — multiple constraints compound
Notes: Multiple constraints compound. Environmental contamination adds remediation cost and schedule. Urban sites limit equipment and staging.

Description: Level of Building Information Modeling required on the project — BIM adoption level correlates with coordination quality and clash detection effectiveness. LOD 300-350 is becoming standard for commercial projects above $25M. LOD 400 for complex MEP. Digital twin for owner-operators who want lifecycle data.
Industry Standard: AIA E203 (BIM Protocol); AGC BIM Education; LOD Specification
Common Values: None, LOD 200 (Design Coordination), LOD 300 (Construction Coordination), LOD 350 (Trade Coordination), LOD 400 (Fabrication), LOD 500 (As-Built), Full Digital Twin
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; BIM platforms (Revit, Navisworks, BIM 360); MS Excel
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. BIM requirements live in project BIM execution plan
Benchmarking Use: BIM adoption level correlates with coordination quality and reduced field conflicts
Notes: LOD 300-350 standard for commercial $25M+. LOD 400 for complex MEP. BIM investment reduces field coordination issues.

Description: Percentage of construction using off-site prefabrication or modular methods — shifts labor from field to factory. Reduces on-site workforce peak but requires earlier procurement and more detailed coordination. Factory-controlled environment typically produces higher quality. Growing trend in healthcare (bathroom pods) and hospitality (modular rooms).
Industry Standard: Modular Building Institute (MBI); Smith Midland; Volumetric Building Companies
Common Values: None, Light Prefab (<15%: MEP racks / wall panels), Moderate Prefab (15-40%: bathroom pods / headwalls), Heavy Prefab / Volumetric Modular (>40%)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Modular fabricators
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Prefab strategy lives in project delivery and procurement plans
Benchmarking Use: Prefab affects schedule compression, workforce peak, and quality metrics
Notes: Shifts labor from field to factory. Reduces on-site peak but requires earlier procurement. Quality typically higher.

Description: Notable specialty building systems that add cost, schedule, and coordination complexity — specialty systems often have proprietary vendors, long lead times, and complex commissioning requirements. A hospital with OR suites, labs, and clean rooms is fundamentally different from a standard office building.
Common Values: Central Plant, Clean Room, Vivarium, Surgical Suite, Commercial Kitchen, Natatorium / Pool, Auditorium, Parking Structure, Data Center / IT, Laboratory Systems, Radiation Shielding, Pneumatic Tube, Nurse Call, DAS
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel; Specialty system vendors
Procore Coverage: None — not tracked in PM systems. Specialty systems identified in design documents and scope narratives
Benchmarking Use: Specialty systems add cost and complexity — proprietary vendors with long lead times and complex commissioning
Notes: A hospital with OR suites, labs, and clean rooms costs 3-5x per SF more than a standard office for MEP alone.

Category: --- PERFORMANCE: project status, phase tracking, and earned value metrics. ---

Description: Current state of the project in its lifecycle — determines what data is available and how the project should be treated in benchmarks. Only closed/archived projects have final cost and schedule data. Active projects have in-progress metrics. Cancelled projects should be excluded from benchmarks.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK Project Lifecycle; Procore project stages
Common Values: Pre-Construction, Active Construction, On Hold, Substantially Complete, Final Completion, Warranty Period, Closed / Archived, Cancelled
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated project status/stage field, widely populated. Status workflow tracks lifecycle progression
Benchmarking Use: Filters for active vs. historical benchmarking — only completed projects have final performance data
Notes: Only closed projects have final data. Active projects show in-progress metrics. Cancelled should be excluded from benchmarks.

Description: Current construction phase based on the phases taxonomy — enables phase-aware analytics and 'where should you be at this point' benchmarks. A project in MEP Rough-In should have a certain percentage of changes processed, RFIs closed, and submittals approved. Links to taxonomy-phases.csv.
Industry Standard: None — derived from project activity patterns
Common Values: Preconstruction, Procurement, Mobilization, Sitework & Foundations, Structure, Building Enclosure, MEP Rough-In, Interior Rough-In, Finishes & MEP Trim, Commissioning, Closeout, Warranty & Operations
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Project; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — no dedicated current phase field in most PM systems. Phase is inferred from schedule, activity patterns, or manual update
Benchmarking Use: Links to phases taxonomy — enables phase-aware analytics and milestone tracking
Notes: Phase classification enables 'where should you be' benchmarks. Requires either manual tagging or inference from activity data.

Description: Overall project completion percentage — multiple methods: cost-based (earned value), milestone-based, or units-based. Cost-based is most common for overall project. The gap between percent complete and percent schedule elapsed indicates schedule health.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK Earned Value Management; AACE EVM
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Project; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — project percent complete field exists but adoption and update frequency vary. Manual entry or derived from schedule
Benchmarking Use: Compare percent complete vs. percent schedule elapsed for schedule health assessment
Notes: Method matters: cost-based, milestone-based, or units-based produce different numbers. Always specify method.

Description: Cost Performance Index — the most reliable predictor of final cost performance. Calculation: BCWP (Budgeted Cost of Work Performed) / ACWP (Actual Cost of Work Performed). CPI < 1.0 means over budget. CPI at 20% complete is the strongest predictor of final cost outcome — it rarely recovers.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK EVM; AACE 10S-90; DoD EVM (ANSI 748)
Common Values: 0.5–1.5 (1.0 = on budget, <1.0 = over budget, >1.0 = under budget)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; EVM tools; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — requires both budget baseline and actual cost tracking linked to schedule. Financial module provides components but EVM calculation is manual
Benchmarking Use: Core EVM metric — CPI at 20% complete is the strongest predictor of final cost outcome
Notes: CPI rarely recovers after 20% complete. Industry-validated predictive metric. Requires disciplined cost and schedule tracking.

Description: Schedule Performance Index — measures schedule efficiency. Calculation: BCWP (Budgeted Cost of Work Performed) / BCWS (Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled). SPI < 1.0 means behind schedule. Less reliable than CPI because schedule recovery is more achievable than cost recovery through acceleration and resequencing.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK EVM; AACE 10S-90; DoD EVM (ANSI 748)
Common Values: 0.5–1.5 (1.0 = on schedule, <1.0 = behind, >1.0 = ahead)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; EVM tools; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — requires both schedule baseline and progress tracking linked to cost. Schedule integration provides components but EVM calculation is manual
Benchmarking Use: Core EVM metric — SPI < 1.0 means behind schedule. Less predictive than CPI because schedule recovery is more achievable
Notes: SPI is less reliable than CPI as a predictor. Schedule recovery is more achievable through acceleration and overtime.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: derived KPIs and performance metrics calculated from project attributes. ---

Description: Percentage increase from contract value to final cost at completion — the most important financial performance metric in construction. Calculation: (Final Cost − Contract Value) / Contract Value × 100. Average is 4-8% for well-managed projects, 15-25% for complex renovations. Source entities: contract value, final cost at completion.
Common Values: 4-8% (well-managed), 10-15% (typical), 15-25% (complex renovation)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires both contract value and final cost. Financial module captures budget vs. committed
Benchmarking Use: The core financial performance metric — compare across project types, delivery methods, and contractors
Notes: Only available on completed projects. Must normalize by work type (new vs. renovation) and delivery method.

Description: Percentage increase from planned to actual duration — the core schedule performance metric. Calculation: (Actual Duration − Planned Duration) / Planned Duration × 100. Average is 10-20% for well-managed projects. Renovation and fast-track projects have higher schedule growth.
Common Values: 5-10% (well-managed), 10-20% (typical), 20-40% (complex renovation)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Primavera P6; MS Project; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires both planned and actual dates, which are standard project fields
Benchmarking Use: Core schedule performance metric — compare across project types, delivery methods, and seasons
Notes: Must normalize by work type and delivery method. Fast-track projects have compressed planned duration, affecting growth calculation.

Description: Total approved change order value as percentage of original contract value — measures scope stability and document quality. Calculation: Total CO Value / Original Contract Value × 100. Industry average is 5-10% for new construction, 15-25% for renovation. Low CO rate may indicate suppressed changes, not good performance.
Common Values: 3-5% (simple new), 5-10% (typical new), 10-15% (complex new), 15-25% (renovation)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — change events and change orders tracked in financial module with structured status and cost fields
Benchmarking Use: Measures scope stability and document quality — compare by delivery method, work type, and A/E firm
Notes: Low CO rate may indicate suppressed changes (scope buried in allowances) rather than good performance.

Description: Number of RFIs per $1M of contract value — measures document quality and design completeness. High RFI density correlates with incomplete documents, aggressive fast-track schedule, or complex design. Calculation: Total RFI Count / (Contract Value / $1M). Source entities: RFIs, contract value.
Common Values: 10-20 RFIs/$1M (good documents), 20-40 (typical), 40+ (poor documents or fast-track)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — RFIs tracked with structured status workflow. Contract value from project attributes
Benchmarking Use: Measures document quality and design completeness — compare by A/E firm and delivery method
Notes: High RFI density = incomplete documents or complex design. DB typically has lower RFI density than DBB.

Description: Total Recordable Incident Rate — OSHA-standard safety metric. Calculation: (Total Recordable Incidents × 200,000) / Total Labor Hours Worked. Industry average is 2.5-3.5 for commercial construction. Lower is better. Leading indicator when tracked alongside observation frequency.
Common Values: 0.5-1.0 (excellent), 1.0-2.5 (good), 2.5-3.5 (average), 3.5+ (poor)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Safety platforms (iAuditor, Predictive Solutions)
Procore Coverage: Medium — safety observations and incidents tracked in PM tools. Labor hours from manpower logs. TRIR calculation is manual
Benchmarking Use: Core safety metric — compare by project type, size, and contractor
Notes: Observation frequency (leading indicator) predicts TRIR (lagging indicator). Projects with higher observation rates have lower TRIR.

Description: Number of punch items per 1000 SF at substantial completion — measures overall quality of installed work. High density indicates quality issues during construction. Typical: 2-5 items per 1000 SF for well-managed projects. Paint/finish is consistently the #1 punch category.
Common Values: 1-2/1000 SF (excellent), 2-5 (typical), 5-10 (poor), 10+ (significant quality issues)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; PlanGrid; Fieldwire; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — punch items tracked with structured status, type, and location fields
Benchmarking Use: Measures overall construction quality at closeout — compare by project type, GC, and trade
Notes: Paint/finish is consistently #1 punch category across all project types. High punch density correlates with longer closeout duration.

Description: Percentage of submittals approved on first submission — measures specification clarity, subcontractor preparation quality, and A/E review efficiency. Calculation: First-Submission Approvals / Total Submittals × 100. Re-submittals compound schedule delay.
Common Values: 70-80% first-pass (good), 60-70% (typical), <60% (poor specs or preparation)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Oracle Aconex; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — submittals tracked with structured status workflow including revision tracking
Benchmarking Use: Measures spec clarity and sub preparation quality — compare by division and A/E firm
Notes: Re-submittals compound schedule delay — each re-sub adds 2-4 weeks. First-pass rate correlates with document quality.
